summary: Jeff Atwood uses the ill-fated Windows Vista ads featuring Bill Gates and Jerry Seinfeld as a springboard to discuss tech advertising and his love of Code Complete. He critiques both Microsoft's opaque ad campaign and Apple's 'I'm a Mac' ads, noting the irony that the PC character played by John Hodgman is actually more likable than the Mac guy. The post pivots to highlight that Gates is shown reading from Code Complete in the ad, which Atwood considers his all-time favorite programming book and the namesake of his blog. He sees Gates' choice of that specific book as intentional and meaningful.
key_points: The Gates/Seinfeld Vista ads were poorly received and possibly cancelled, though they succeeded in generating conversation
Apple's Mac vs PC ads backfire somewhat because John Hodgman (the PC) is more likable and interesting than Justin Long (the Mac)
Advertising is fundamentally deceptive - Hodgman actually uses Macs in real life
Gates reading Code Complete in the ad was likely a deliberate choice reflecting his appreciation for the book
The quote Gates reads argues that inheritance and polymorphism don't automatically make a design good
money_quotes: The goal of advertising is to get people talking about it. Even if every one of those conversations starts with 'what the hell were they thinking,' hey – it's a conversation.
I'd much sooner associate myself with Hodgman than with Long.
The sleight of hand breaks down a bit when you realize that Hodgman actually uses Macs, but that's advertising for you: a giant pack of lies.
The fact that a design uses inheritance and polymorphism doesn't make it a good design.
On the whole, I'd call these ads opaque bordering on inane.
key_insight: Even failed advertising campaigns can contain nuggets of genuine value, like Bill Gates quietly endorsing one of the best programming books ever written while the internet debates whether the ads make any sense.
